narration: Define a recursive function that calls itself to perform repeated tasks.
Explain that recursion is useful for traversing tree data structures due to its multiplying nature.
Describe how a function calling itself twice creates a tree-like structure of function calls.
Define a base case as an if condition that prevents infinite recursive calls.
Explain that missing a base case causes a stack overflow and application crash.
Create a variable counter to track the number of function calls.
Increment the counter variable before the function calls itself.
Set the base case condition to check if the counter is greater than four.
Use the return keyword in the base case to exit the function before the sixth call.
Explain that the call stack piles functions on top of each other, with the most recent at the top.
Modify the function to return the result of the recursive call it invokes.
Explain that returning the recursive call allows the bottom-most function to pass the value back up the stack.
Define memoization as caching a function's return value based on its input parameters.
Explain that memoization avoids recalculating values for inputs that have been processed before.
Create a function that adds 10 to an input value and returns the result.
Create an object to store the cache data for the function.
Add an if condition to check if the input value exists in the cache object.
Return the cached value if the input exists in the cache object.
Store the calculated value in the cache object before returning it.
Wrap the memoization logic in an Immediately Invoked Function Expression (IIFE) to create a closure.
Initialize the cache object inside the closure to keep it private from global scope.
Return the nested function from the closure so it can access the private cache.
Define a function to calculate the Fibonacci sequence using a standard loop.
Initialize two variables to hold the first two values of the Fibonacci sequence (0 and 1).
Add a base case to return the input n if it is less than 2.
Create a for loop that starts at index 2 and runs until index n.
Calculate the next Fibonacci number by summing the previous two numbers.
Update the previous two number variables to move the sequence forward.
Define a recursive function for the Fibonacci sequence.
Set the base case to return n if n is less than 2.
Return the sum of the function called with n-1 and the function called with n-2.
Explain that the recursive Fibonacci algorithm has exponential time complexity (2^n).
Implement memoization into the recursive Fibonacci function using a closure.
Check the cache object for the requested index before performing calculations.
Store the calculated Fibonacci number in the cache object indexed by n.
Explain that memoization reduces the time complexity to linear time.
Define a function to calculate the factorial of a number using a loop.
Initialize a result variable to 1.
Create a loop that multiplies the result by the loop counter until the counter reaches n.
Define a recursive function for the factorial algorithm.
Set the base case to return 2 if n is equal to 2.
Return n multiplied by the function called with n-1.
Explain that memoization is less beneficial for the factorial algorithm since it is already linear time.
